# Processes, Permissions, and Secrets

The last three chapters treated files as things to read, search,
and transform. This one is about the questions that surround a
file rather than its contents: what is *running* on your
machine, *who* is allowed to read a given file, and where a
*secret* you type can quietly end up. Those look like three
subjects, and the chapter is organized as three, but they are
one idea seen from three sides. A permission decides who may
read a file. A process is a running program that exposes, to
anyone who can list processes, the exact command line that
started it. And a secret leaks precisely where these meet: it
sits in a file whose permissions you set, it rides in the
arguments of a process anyone can see, and it is copied into a
history file and a log whether you meant it or not.

This is the chapter where the pieces connect. Chapter 2
introduced the environment and `export`; here that becomes the
way you hand a program a password without writing it down.
Chapter 5 taught you to *read* the `rwx` permission string on a
file; here you learn to *set* it, which Chapter 5 explicitly
left for this chapter. Chapter 7 taught that the shell can
destroy what Git never saw; the sibling danger here is that the
shell can *reveal* what you meant to keep quiet, and it is just
as irreversible, because a secret that has been shown cannot be
un-shown, only rotated. The companion Git book owns the rule
that a credential must never be committed; this chapter owns the
rest of the credential's life on your machine: keeping it out of
your shell history, out of the process list, out of your logs,
and readable only by you.

A note on where the output comes from. The background jobs,
signals, `nohup`, `chmod`, environment inheritance, the `ps`
argument leak, and the `.gitignore` read are executed in this
book's Linux sandbox (Ubuntu 22.04.5, GNU bash 5.1.16, GNU
coreutils 8.32, procps-ng 3.3.17, Python 3.10.12; the `ch10-*`
transcripts), in throwaway `/tmp` scratch directories so nothing
real is touched. Two things run on the author's Mac
(`transcripts/capture-ch10-mac.sh`): `chown` ownership, because
changing a file's owner needs privileges the sandbox account
does not have, and BSD `ps`, to confirm the argument exposure is
not a Linux-only quirk. Shell history is a per-shell interactive
feature; its bash form is shown from the sandbox and its zsh form
is pinned to the zsh manual, because an interactive zsh on a real
terminal reads the keyboard, not a script's piped input. Every
secret value in this chapter is a fake placeholder, never a real
credential, and the account name that `ls -l` and `id` would
print is masked to `[account]`.

## Running a job in the background: `&` and `jobs`

Most commands run in the *foreground*: you type them, they run,
and the prompt comes back when they finish. That is fine until a
command takes minutes or hours, such as regenerating the data or
running the whole pipeline, because it ties up the terminal the
whole time. Ending a command with an ampersand, `&`, runs it in
the *background* instead: the shell starts it and hands you the
prompt back immediately. A long `sleep` stands in here for a
slow `make`:

```bash
$ sleep 30 &
$ echo "$!"
7
$ jobs
[1]+  Running                 sleep 30 &
$ kill %1
$ jobs
```

Four things happen in that block. The `&` launched `sleep` in
the background and returned the prompt at once. `$!` is a shell
variable holding the process ID (PID) of the most recent
background job, `7` here, which you save when you need to refer
to the job later. `jobs` lists the background jobs of the
current shell, with a bracketed *job number* (`[1]`) that
belongs to this shell, not to the whole machine. And `kill %1`
ends the job by its job number; the final `jobs` prints nothing
because the job is gone. (At an interactive prompt your shell
also prints a `[1] 12345` line when a job starts and a
`[1]+ Done` or `Terminated` line when it ends; the book's Linux
captures run non-interactively, which suppresses those two
notices, but `jobs` shows the same state.)

When you do want to wait for a background job before moving on,
`wait` blocks until it finishes:

```bash
$ sleep 2 & echo "started $!"
started 9
$ wait
$ echo "all background jobs done"
all background jobs done
```

That pattern, launch several jobs with `&` and then `wait`, is
how you run independent steps at once and reconvene before the
step that needs them all. A background job is a real operating
system process, and `ps` shows it as one, with its PID, its
parent's PID, its state, and the command that started it:

```bash
$ sleep 300 &
$ ps -o pid,ppid,stat,args -p "$!"
    PID    PPID STAT COMMAND
      7       5 S    sleep 300
```

`ps` reports on processes the way `ls` reports on files. The
`-o` flag chooses which columns to print (here PID, parent PID,
state, and the full command), and `-p "$!"` restricts it to the
one process we just backgrounded. The `S` state means sleeping,
which is what a `sleep` should be. Two interactive controls
round out the picture and are worth knowing by name even though
they need a live terminal to try: Ctrl-Z *suspends* the
foreground job and hands back the prompt, `bg` resumes that
suspended job in the background, and `fg` brings a background job
back to the foreground. They are the interactive cousins of the
`&` you type up front.

Backgrounding with `&` keeps a job alive only as long as the
shell that owns it. Close the terminal and the job usually dies
with it, which is the problem `nohup` addresses two sections
down, and which tmux (Chapter 15) will solve properly for jobs
you want to detach from and reattach to later, including on a
remote server (Chapter 14). For now the job lives and dies with
this shell.

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
`ps` is one of the commands where the macOS (BSD) and Linux
(GNU/procps) versions grew apart, mostly in how you ask for a
listing. The `ps aux` form, which lists every process with its
user, PID, CPU and memory use, and command, is BSD-style and
works on both platforms, so it is the portable one to memorize.
The `ps -ef` form prints similar information in the System V
style and is the common Linux idiom, but older BSD `ps` did not
accept it. The column selector used above, `ps -o pid,args`,
works on both. On the author's Mac the same `ps -o pid,args`
prints the identical exposed command line
(`transcripts/ch10-ps-mac.txt`). Prefer `ps aux` (or `ps -o` with
explicit columns) when you want a line that runs unchanged
whether you are on your laptop or SSH'd into a Linux box.
:::

## Signals and `kill`: ending a job well

`kill` does not necessarily kill. It sends a *signal*, a small
numbered message the operating system delivers to a process, and
the process decides how to respond. The default signal is
`SIGTERM` (terminate), a polite request to shut down that a
program can catch in order to finish writing a file, remove a
scratch directory, or otherwise clean up before it exits. A
short script that traps `SIGTERM` shows the courtesy at work:

```bash
$ ./longjob.sh &
$ kill "$!"          # default signal is SIGTERM
$ cat job.log
job started (pid 10)
caught SIGTERM, cleaning up scratch files
```

The script installed a handler with `trap cleanup TERM`, so when
the plain `kill` sent `SIGTERM`, the handler ran and wrote its
cleanup line before exiting. Compare the impatient version. The
`-9` signal is `SIGKILL`, which the kernel enforces directly and
which a process cannot catch, block, or clean up after:

```bash
$ ./longjob.sh &
$ kill -9 "$!"       # SIGKILL cannot be caught
$ cat job.log
job started (pid 17)
```

Same script, same start, but the log ends after "started": the
process was cut down mid-stride with no chance to run its
handler, so the cleanup never happened. That is the whole
difference between the two signals, and it is why reaching for
`kill -9` first is a bad habit rather than a power move.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
`kill -9` (`SIGKILL`) is not "kill harder," it is "kill without
letting the program clean up." A process killed with `-9` cannot
flush a half-written file, remove a lock, or delete a temp
directory, so `-9` is how you end up with a corrupt output or a
stale lock file that blocks the next run. The habit that avoids
it: send the default `SIGTERM` first (plain `kill "$PID"` or
`kill %1`), give the process a moment, and only escalate to
`kill -9` if it genuinely refuses to exit. Save `SIGKILL` for a
process that is truly stuck, not as your opening move. You end a
job by its job number (`kill %1`) or its PID (`kill "$PID"`);
`$!` gives you the PID of the job you just backgrounded, and
`ps` or `jobs` finds the others.
:::

## Keeping a job alive after logout: `nohup` (ADVANCED) {.tier-advanced}

When a terminal closes, the shell sends its jobs a `SIGHUP`
(hang up), and the default response to `SIGHUP` is to die. On
your laptop that is usually what you want. On a remote server it
is a real hazard: start a long job over SSH, lose the connection
or close the lid, and the job receives `SIGHUP` and stops
halfway. `nohup` runs a command with `SIGHUP` ignored, so it
survives the terminal going away:

```bash
$ nohup ./long_job.sh > build.log 2>&1 &
$ cat build.log
step 1 done
step 2 done
step 3 done
```

The `nohup` at the front makes the job deaf to `SIGHUP`; the
`> build.log 2>&1` sends its output to a file (Chapter 7's
redirection) so nothing is lost when the terminal that would
have displayed it is gone; and the `&` backgrounds it. Redirect
explicitly like this: left to itself, `nohup` dumps output into
a file called `nohup.out` in the current directory, which is
easy to overwrite and hard to find. A companion is `disown`,
which removes an already-running background job from the shell's
job table so it is not sent `SIGHUP` when the shell exits; you
reach for it when you forgot `nohup` and the job is already
running.

`nohup` is a stopgap, not a workflow. It keeps a job alive
through a disconnect, but you cannot reattach to it to see how it
is doing; the output only appears in the log. The tool built for
running long jobs you detach from and later reattach to, watching
them live, is tmux, which Chapter 15 will cover, and which is the
right answer for serious remote work (Chapter 14). Use `nohup`
for fire-and-forget; use tmux when you want to come back and
look.

## Permissions: `chmod` on the files that matter

Chapter 5 taught you to read the permission string that `ls -l`
prints: a type character and then three `rwx` triples for the
file's owner, its group, and everyone else, each showing read,
write, and execute in turn. This section is the other half of
that skill, changing those bits with `chmod`, which Chapter 5
deferred to here. There are two ways to spell a permission
change, and you will meet both.

The *symbolic* form names who and what: `u` (user/owner), `g`
(group), `o` (other), or `a` (all), then `+`, `-`, or `=`, then
the bits. Making a script executable is the everyday case:

```bash
$ ls -l deploy.sh
-rw-r--r-- 1 [account] [account] 28 Jul  3 19:32 deploy.sh
$ chmod u+x deploy.sh
$ ls -l deploy.sh
-rwxr--r-- 1 [account] [account] 28 Jul  3 19:32 deploy.sh
```

`chmod u+x` added (`+`) the execute bit (`x`) for the owner
(`u`), and the owner triple went from `rw-` to `rwx`. The
*octal* form sets all nine bits at once with three digits, one
each for owner, group, and other, where read is 4, write is 2,
and execute is 1, summed per digit: `644` is `rw-r--r--` (owner
6 = 4+2, group and other 4), and `755` is `rwxr-xr-x`. The one
octal number worth committing to memory is `600`, owner reads
and writes, nobody else can even read, which is exactly what a
file holding a secret should be:

```bash
$ ls -l .env
-rw-r--r-- 1 [account] [account] 31 Jul  3 19:32 .env
$ chmod 600 .env
$ ls -l .env
-rw------- 1 [account] [account] 31 Jul  3 19:32 .env
```

The `.env` file (the next section's subject) started as
`rw-r--r--`, readable by everyone on the machine, and `chmod
600` closed it to `rw-------`, readable and writable only by its
owner. On a shared server that difference is the difference
between a credential only you can read and one every other
account can. This is where permissions stop being trivia and
start being security, and it is the direct tie between this
section and the secrets ones that follow: a key file is only as
private as its permission bits.

::: {.callout-caution .danger title="DANGER"}
`chmod 777` (or `chmod -R 777`) makes a file or a whole tree
readable, writable, *and* executable by every account on the
machine. It is the reflexive "fix" for a permission error, and
it is almost always wrong: on a shared server it hands your
files, including anything sensitive, to everyone, and a
world-writable file is one any other user can quietly replace
with their own contents. The world-writable bit on a directory
is worse, because it lets another user delete or swap the files
inside it. When a program cannot read a file, the right move is
to grant the *minimum* that fixes it, usually restoring your own
access with `chmod u+rw` or `600`/`644`, never opening it to the
world. If you ever see `chmod -R 777` in an instruction, treat it
the way Chapter 7 treats a blind `curl | sh`: a red flag, not a
recipe.
:::

## Ownership: `chown` and `chgrp` (ADVANCED) {.tier-advanced}

Permissions decide what the owner, the group, and others may do;
*ownership* decides who those first two are. Every file has an
owning user and an owning group, the two names `ls -l` prints
between the permission string and the size. `chown` changes the
user, `chgrp` (or `chown :group`) changes the group, and the
combined form `chown user:group` sets both at once. Changing a
file's *group* to one you already belong to is something you can
do to your own files; it is how you hand a file to a shared
project group on a server so the group's `rwx` triple applies to
your collaborators.

Changing a file's *owner* to a different user is another matter,
because it is privileged: if you could give your files away, or
worse, take ownership of someone else's, the permission system
would mean nothing. So an ordinary account cannot do it, and the
attempt fails cleanly (captured on the author's Mac,
`transcripts/ch10-chown-mac.txt`):

```bash
$ chown "$(id -un)" key.pem      # to yourself: allowed
$ chown daemon key.pem           # to another user: denied
chown: key.pem: Operation not permitted
$ echo $?
1
```

Handing the file back to yourself is a legal no-op that shows
the syntax; handing it to `daemon`, another user, is refused
with "Operation not permitted" and a nonzero exit status.
Changing owner across users is a job for an administrator with
`sudo`, which this book does not run on your behalf. For a
researcher the practical shape of this is simple: you set
*permissions* (`chmod`) on your own files constantly, you change
their *group* (`chgrp`) occasionally to share on a server, and
you rarely change *owner* at all, because that is the
administrator's lever, not yours.

## Secrets in environment variables

A research script often needs a credential: an API key for a
data provider, a database password, a token for a compute
service. The wrong place for it is inside the script, because the
script goes into Git and the companion Git book's rule is
absolute, a credential must never be committed. Chapter 2's
environment is the standard place to put it instead. You
`export` the secret into an environment variable, and the
program reads it from there, so the value lives in the running
shell's memory and never in a tracked file:

```bash
$ export API_KEY=sk-not-a-real-key-1234
$ python3 readkey.py &
```

The script reads `os.environ["API_KEY"]`; the key is never
written in the code. That `export` line is shown with a fake key
to demonstrate one thing only, how a program picks a value up
from the environment. Do not set a *real* key this way: typing
`export API_KEY=<the actual value>` puts the secret on a command
line, which the next section shows is exactly how it lands in
your shell history. For a real credential you load the value
without ever typing it: keep it in a `600` `.env` file and pull
it into the environment with `source .env` or a loader (the
filename is typed, the secret is not), read it from a hidden
prompt (`read -s API_KEY`), or fetch it from a password manager.
Projects collect these in a `.env` file that is loaded into the
environment but never committed, and the running example already
anticipates this. Its `.gitignore`, following the rule Chapter 6
set, names the secret files explicitly so they cannot be added
by accident:

```bash
$ sed -n '23,26p' .gitignore
# --- Secrets: never commit credentials ---
.env
*.pem
secrets.*
```

That stanza ignores the `.env` file, any `.pem` key, and
anything starting with `secrets.`, which is why `chmod 600` on
that `.env` (the previous section) and this `.gitignore` entry
are two halves of one habit: the file is readable only by you and
untracked by Git. The environment keeps a secret out of your
source, but it is not a vault, and its one sharp edge is worth a
callout.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
An exported environment variable is inherited by *every* child
process the shell starts, which is the point when it is a `PATH`
and a hazard when it is a secret. A plain shell variable stays in
the shell; an exported one is copied into every command you run
from that shell:

```bash
$ DB_PASSWORD=hunter2
$ bash -c 'echo "child sees: [$DB_PASSWORD]"'
child sees: []
$ export DB_PASSWORD
$ bash -c 'echo "child sees: [$DB_PASSWORD]"'
child sees: [hunter2]
```

Before the `export` the child saw nothing; after it, the child
sees the value. That reach includes programs you did not write
and may not fully trust, and it includes an AI agent you launch
from the same shell, which inherits every secret in your
environment along with everything else (Chapter 17). Export a
secret into the environment only for as long as the task needs
it, prefer a `.env` you load deliberately over exporting keys in
your startup file where they sit in every shell you open, and
remember that "it is in an environment variable" means "every
child process can read it," not "it is hidden."
:::

## Where a secret leaks: history, `ps`, and logs

Keeping a secret out of Git and in a `600` file still leaves
several ways for it to escape, and they all come from typing it
on a command line. The sharpest is the process list. A program's
command-line arguments are visible, through `ps`, to anyone with
an account on the machine, so a secret passed as an argument is a
secret published to every other user:

```bash
$ python3 -c 'import time; time.sleep(30)' --password=hunter2 &
$ ps -o pid,args -p "$!"
    PID COMMAND
     22 python3 -c import time; time.sleep(30) --password=hunter2
```

The fake password is sitting in plain sight in the `COMMAND`
column, and on a shared server any user could run `ps aux` and
read it.

::: {.callout-caution .danger title="DANGER"}
A secret typed on a command line leaks three ways at once, and
every one of them outlives the command. It is written to your
shell history file, where it sits on disk for months. It is
exposed in the process list, where `ps` shows it to every other
account on the machine for as long as the process runs. And if
its output or the command itself reaches the screen, it is copied
into any log, scrollback, screenshot, or agent transcript that
happens to be recording. This is the reveal-it sibling of Chapter
7's destroy-it danger, and it is just as irreversible: a secret
that has been shown cannot be un-shown. The rest of this section
is the defenses, and they share one root, do not put the secret
on the command line: read it from an environment variable or a
`600` file, and it is never typed, never in `ps`, and never in
history to begin with.
:::

The fix is the previous section's mechanism: have the program
read the secret from the environment or a file, never from an
argument, so the argument list carries nothing sensitive:

```bash
$ export API_KEY=sk-not-a-real-key-1234
$ python3 readkey.py &
$ ps -o pid,args -p "$!"
    PID COMMAND
      8 python3 readkey.py
```

Same kind of program, but its arguments are just `readkey.py`;
the key it uses came from the environment and never appears in
`ps`. Moving the secret into the environment closes the `ps`
argv leak, but the environment is not a vault: as the previous
section's PITFALL showed, every child process inherits it, and
anyone who can inspect your processes or read `/proc/<pid>/environ`
for a process running as you can still see it. Environment
variables are the right place to hand a secret to a program, not
a place to keep one safe at rest; a `600` file is stronger for
that, and neither excuses leaving the value in history. The
second leak is your own shell history. Every command
you run is appended to a history file (`$HISTFILE`, typically
`~/.zsh_history` or `~/.bash_history`), so a secret typed on the
command line is written to disk and sits there for months. The
shell offers a deliberate way to keep a command out of history:
begin the line with a space. With the right option set
(`HISTCONTROL=ignorespace` in bash, `setopt HIST_IGNORE_SPACE`
in zsh), a space-prefixed command is not recorded. Running a few
commands with that option set, one of them a secret typed with a
leading space, and then inspecting a throwaway history file
shows the secret line simply absent (this book's Linux sandbox
runs bash, `transcripts/ch10-history.txt`):

```bash
$ cat bash_history_demo
echo "public setup line"
set +o history
echo "public line after"
```

The saved file holds only the public lines. The secret command,
a leading-space `curl` carrying a bearer token, never reached
the file, and neither did the command run in the middle: for a
stretch of commands you can turn recording off entirely with
`set +o history` and back on with `set -o history`, which is why
that toggle appears but the work between the toggles does not.
These help, but the surest habit is not to type the secret at
all: read it from a `600` file or an environment variable, so it
is never on a command line to be recorded in the first place.

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
The leading-space trick needs an option whose name differs by
shell, and it is off by default either way. The demo above ran
in bash, where the option is `HISTCONTROL=ignorespace` (or
`ignoreboth`, which also drops duplicate lines). On macOS your
interactive shell is zsh, where the same behavior is a shell
option you turn on with `setopt HIST_IGNORE_SPACE`; the zsh
manual describes it as removing "command lines from the history
list when the first character on the line is a space." The
mechanism and the habit are identical, only the setting's name
differs, and this is a genuine shell divergence rather than a
BSD-versus-GNU one, since it is the shell, not a coreutil, that
keeps the history. Set whichever option matches your shell in
your startup file (Chapter 18) if you want the leading-space
habit always available, and do not assume a machine has it on:
when in doubt, keep the secret off the command line entirely.
:::

The third set of leaks is quieter because it does not feel like
the terminal at all. A secret printed to the screen is copied
into any log you are keeping with `tee` or `script` (Chapter
11), into the scrollback your terminal saves, into a screenshot
you paste into a chat or an issue, and into the transcript an AI
agent keeps of your session (Chapter 17, which is why you never
paste a key into an agent's prompt). None of these is malicious;
they are ordinary conveniences that happen to be permanent. The
rule that covers all of them is the same one the process list
taught: do not put the secret where it can be captured, which
means not typing it and not printing it.

::: {.callout-tip .recovery title="RECOVERY"}
If a secret has already leaked, into a committed file, a shell
history, a shared log, a screenshot, or an agent transcript, the
recovery is not to delete the copy, because you can rarely find
every copy and deletion does not help against anyone who already
read it. The only real fix is to *rotate* the credential:
generate a new key or password at the provider, update wherever
the real service reads it, and revoke the old one, which makes
the leaked value worthless. Treat any secret that has touched a
command line, a screen, or a repository as compromised and rotate
it; a leaked key is a key to replace, not a mistake to hide.
Then remove the file from Git's future (the companion Git book
covers purging it from history) and fix the habit that put it on
the command line, so the next one does not leak the same way.
:::

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

These use the asset-pricing project from Chapter 6. Use fake
values for every "secret," never a real credential, and do the
throwaway steps in `/tmp`.

1. Background a long job. Run `sleep 60 &`, capture its PID with
   `echo "$!"`, list it with `jobs` and inspect it with
   `ps -o pid,stat,args -p "$PID"`, then end it with `kill %1`.
   Confirm with `jobs` that it is gone.

2. Compare the two signals. Write a short script that traps
   `SIGTERM` and writes a "cleaning up" line to a log before it
   exits, then run it in the background and end it once with a
   plain `kill` and once with `kill -9`. Explain, from the two
   logs, why `kill -9` is not your first choice.

3. Lock down a key file. In a scratch directory create a fake
   `.env` (`echo 'API_KEY=sk-fake-0000' > .env`), read its
   permissions with `ls -l`, set them to `600` with `chmod`, and
   confirm the group and other triples are now empty. Explain who
   could read it before and after.

4. Leak a secret into `ps`, then fix it. Start a background
   process that takes a fake `--token=...` argument and find the
   token with `ps -o pid,args`. Then set the value in an
   environment variable instead and confirm `ps` no longer shows
   it. Which of the two would you use on a shared server?

5. Keep a secret out of history. In your own shell, set the
   leading-space option for your shell (`setopt HIST_IGNORE_SPACE`
   in zsh, `HISTCONTROL=ignorespace` in bash), run a harmless
   command with a leading space, and confirm with `history` that
   it was not recorded. Articulate why reading the value from a
   file would be safer still.

6. Trace the secret files. Open the project's `.gitignore` and
   find the stanza that ignores `.env`, `*.pem`, and `secrets.*`.
   Explain how that entry and a `chmod 600` on the same `.env`
   are two halves of one habit, and name one place a secret could
   still leak that neither of them covers.
